pathophysiology_summary: Long-standing poorly controlled diabetes has caused profound sensorimotor and autonomic neuropathy with loss of protective sensation, foot deformity (Charcot) and dry autonomically-denervated skin. Repetitive unfelt pressure over a bony prominence (first metatarsal head) produced callus and then a full-thickness plantar (neuropathic) ulcer, now infected (malodorous seropurulent discharge, perilesional erythema). Because pain and fever are blunted, the wound and infection are silently advanced, with real risk of deep-space infection, osteomyelitis (a probe-to-bone or deep wound raises this) and sepsis/limb loss. Discriminators: bounding pedal pulses and a warm foot indicate a neuropathic (not ischaemic/arterial) ulcer; the plantar weight-bearing location with callus is against a venous ulcer; the classic pressure-point neuropathic aetiology is against a Marjolin's/SCC. This is a diabetic-foot emergency requiring urgent in-person High-Risk Foot Service/ED assessment, offloading, antibiotics, imaging for osteomyelitis, and glycaemic optimisation — not remote dressing advice.

differential.evidence_basis: Painless deep plantar ulcer with callus and malodorous discharge in a neuropathic diabetic.
A deep chronic diabetic foot ulcer over bone must be evaluated for underlying bone infection.
Bounding pulses and a warm painless foot argue against ischaemia (though diabetes can be mixed).
Rare in a chronic wound; the classic pressure-point neuropathic picture makes mechanical/neuropathic aetiology far more likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a limb-threatening infected neuropathic ulcer, screen for osteomyelitis and sepsis, and escalate urgently — do not be reassured by painlessness.

differential.diagnosis: Infected neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer (± osteomyelitis)
differential.evidence_basis: Deep infected neuropathic ulcer; systemic sepsis features currently absent but osteomyelitis cannot be excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Infected neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer — limb-threatening
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis: urgent in-person High-Risk Foot/ED assessment, antibiotics, offloading, imaging for osteomyelitis, glycaemic control.

red_flags.description: Deep painless plantar ulcer with loss of protective sensation (neuropathic, silently advancing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Loss of protective sensation means ongoing silent trauma and delayed presentation — high amputation risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued weight-bearing and infection lead to deep destruction and limb loss.

red_flags.description: Local infection (malodorous seropurulent discharge, perilesional erythema); possible osteomyelitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Infected diabetic foot ulcer with a deep wound risks osteomyelitis and rapid deterioration.

red_flags.description: Systemic sepsis or critical ischaemia (fever/chills/confusion, ascending redness, blackening of the foot)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate sepsis/gangrene requiring emergency admission.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Sepsis or gangrene with limb/life threat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a painless wound as minor and advising a simple dressing, missing the limb-threatening infection.
Prescribing antibiotics but not arranging offloading and urgent High-Risk Foot/ED assessment or imaging.
Not recognising that a deep chronic ulcer needs evaluation (probe-to-bone, X-ray) for bone infection.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment via a High-Risk Foot Service or ED; empirical antibiotics for the infection, strict offloading and no barefoot walking, and imaging for osteomyelitis; ED/ambulance if sepsis or gangrene features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Remote dressing/antibiotic advice with routine follow-up underplays a diabetic-foot emergency and risks amputation.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Infected diabetic foot ulcers require urgent multidisciplinary (High-Risk Foot Service) assessment, offloading, wound care, appropriate antibiotics, imaging for osteomyelitis, and glycaemic optimisation.
